description: A stunning small tree with magenta-pink flowers that bloom before the leaves emerge in spring. One of the earliest flowering native trees, providing critical early nectar for pollinators and migrants.
An iconic prairie wildflower with rosy-purple petals surrounding a spiky orange cone. Beloved by pollinators in bloom and by goldfinches and other seed-eaters whose seed heads persist through winter.

A multi-season native shrub with white spring flowers, edible blue-purple berries in summer, and brilliant orange-red fall color. One of the highest-value native shrubs for birds — used by over 30 species.
Cheerful golden-yellow flowers with dark centers. A tough, drought-tolerant wildflower native to prairies and open woods. Seed heads feed finches and sparrows well into winter.
A vigorous native climbing vine with spectacular orange-red tubular flowers. The premier hummingbird magnet in the eastern US; also attracts Baltimore and Orchard Orioles seeking nectar.
A loose, arching shrub that becomes spectacular in fall when clusters of vivid magenta-purple berries line the stems. Provides essential fall and winter food for over 40 bird species.

One of eastern North America's most beloved native trees, with white or pink bracts in spring and bright red berries in fall. The berries are a critical high-fat food for migrating birds in September.
Shaggy red to pink flower heads that hummingbirds can't resist. Aromatic foliage repels deer. Seed heads feed sparrows and finches in fall. Thrives in moist sites with full sun.
Nodding red-and-yellow spurred flowers perfectly shaped for hummingbirds and long-tongued bees. One of the earliest spring nectar sources. Thrives in woodland edges and rocky slopes in dappled shade.

A tall, airy native warm-season grass with an architectural quality in the landscape. Seed heads persist through winter feeding juncos, sparrows, and finches. Also provides nesting cover for ground-nesting birds.

A majestic, fast-growing native oak with brilliant red fall color. Research by Doug Tallamy shows oaks support more caterpillar species than any other native tree genus — making them the #1 bird-supporting plant.
A fragrant native shrub of moist woodlands. Tiny yellow flowers emerge in early spring before leaves. Scarlet berries in fall are a critical migration fuel for Swainson's Thrush and Wood Thrush.
A native vine with distinctive 5-leaflet compound leaves that turn brilliant scarlet in fall. Small dark blue-black berries feed woodpeckers, thrushes, waxwings, and over 35 other bird species.

A fast-growing native shrub with large flat-topped clusters of white flowers and dark purple-black berries. Over 50 bird species recorded feeding on the berries, making it among the most productive native fruiting shrubs.
Blazing scarlet spikes up to 4 feet tall; the single best hummingbird plant in the eastern US. Blooms in late summer when other nectar sources are waning. Requires consistently moist soil.
Essential host plant for Monarch butterflies and a top nectar source for many insects. Fragrant pink-lavender flower clusters attract orioles, catbirds, and others hunting nectar-feeding insects.

Vivid orange flower clusters on drought-tolerant stems. Unlike most milkweeds, it doesn't spread aggressively. Attracts hummingbirds, orioles, and a rich community of insectivorous birds hunting pollinators.

A powerhouse native wildflower with arching plumes of tiny yellow flowers in late summer. Supports hundreds of insect species, which in turn attract warblers and other insectivorous birds during fall migration.

Tall, showy native aster with vibrant purple-pink flowers in fall. One of the last flowers of the season, critical for migrating butterflies and late-season birds. Seed heads feed goldfinches and sparrows into winter.
A fast-growing native tree that is second only to oak in the number of caterpillar species it hosts. Small, bitter black cherries ripen in late summer and are consumed by over 40 bird species.
A tough, adaptable native tree recognized by its corky, warty bark. Small orange-red to purple berries persist into winter, feeding Cedar Waxwings, robins, mockingbirds, and many others.
A stately evergreen tree with glossy, spiny leaves and brilliant red berries that persist through winter. The berries become palatable after freezing and provide critical food during hard weather.

A wetland shrub with spherical white pincushion flowers beloved by pollinators. Nutlets produced in round clusters are consumed by waterfowl and shorebirds; the shrub provides important nesting cover.
A multi-season shrub prized for edible berries that are equally loved by over 90 bird species. White urn-shaped flowers in spring are visited by early bumblebees. Spectacular scarlet fall color.
A tough, adaptable native shrub with flat-topped white flower clusters in late spring and dark blue-black berries in late summer. Berries are consumed by over 35 bird species during fall migration.
A native shrub with striking scarlet twigs that illuminate winter landscapes. White berries provide important late-summer food for catbirds, robins, and waxwings. Tolerates wet soils.
A tough native shrub with distinctive exfoliating layered bark and clusters of white or pink flowers. Provides nesting cover, and the seed capsules attract goldfinches, chickadees, and nuthatches.

A majestic native perennial reaching 5–7 feet with large domed clusters of dusty pink-purple flowers beloved by swallowtails and other large butterflies. Attracts insectivorous birds hunting these pollinators.
A statuesque prairie wildflower with intensely vivid purple flowers that stand out in the late-summer landscape. Seed heads attract goldfinches and other seed-eaters from September through winter.

A beautiful native warm-season grass that turns rich russet and copper in fall, holding its fluffy white seed plumes through winter. One of the most wildlife-valuable native grasses, hosting dozens of moth caterpillars.
An elegant, fine-textured native grass forming dense tufts of bright green foliage that turn golden in fall. Fragrant flowers; seeds attract sparrows, juncos, and other ground-feeding birds.
The largest conifer in the northeastern US. Its long, soft needles provide dense nesting and roosting cover. Cones attract Red-breasted Nuthatches, chickadees, and crossbills seeking seeds.

The tallest native tree in the eastern US, with distinctive tulip-shaped flowers in spring and flat, winged seeds. Flowers produce abundant nectar for hummingbirds and orioles. One of the best insect-supporting trees.
A fragrant native tree with mitten-shaped leaves turning vivid orange and red in fall. Dark blue berries on bright red stalks are a preferred food of Eastern Kingbirds, catbirds, and thrushes.
A native shrub or small tree with spectacular fall color and upright cones of fuzzy dark red berries that persist through winter. Berries are consumed by over 30 bird species during cold months.

Dramatic large shrub with velvety antler-like branches and bold compound leaves turning scarlet in fall. Dark red berry clusters persist into winter; particularly valuable for wintering Bluebirds and Robins.

A fast-growing native shrub with fragrant spikes of purple flowers and orange anthers. Produces winged seed pods consumed by goldfinches and other seed-eaters. Extremely adaptable to wet or dry conditions.

A native twining vine with tubular scarlet flowers tipped with yellow inside — perfectly shaped for hummingbirds. Red berries in fall attract thrushes. Unlike invasive Japanese Honeysuckle, this is well-behaved.

A vigorous native grape vine producing small, tart berries relished by over 90 bird species during fall migration. Provides dense nesting cover. An important component of floodplain and edge habitats.

A long-lived prairie perennial with spikes of deep blue-violet flowers and attractive blue-green foliage. Inflated seed pods rattle in the wind and are eaten by seed-eating birds. Deep taproot makes it drought-tolerant.
A bold prairie wildflower reaching 6–8 feet with leaves that form water-collecting cups around the stem — birds drink from these. Yellow daisy flowers attract insects; seed heads attract goldfinches.
Tall spikes of rich blue flowers in late summer; a companion to Cardinal Flower in moist habitats. Attracts hummingbirds and long-tongued bumblebees. Excellent for rain gardens and wet areas.

Lavender-pink blooms attract hummingbirds, hummingbird moths, and a wealth of native bees. More drought-tolerant than Red Bee Balm. Seed heads sought by American Goldfinches and other finches in fall.

A massive, fast-growing native tree of floodplains. Baltimore Orioles weave their distinctive hanging nests in cottonwood branches. Catkins in spring provide early season pollen and caterpillar habitat.
A massive native tree recognized by its patchwork white and tan peeling bark. Large cavity trees are used by Wood Ducks, owls, and Chimney Swifts. Supports many caterpillar species.

A tough, thicket-forming native shrub with clusters of white berries that ripen in late summer. Among the most productive shrubs for migrating birds — robins, thrushes, and waxwings strip the berries in days.
A vigorous native woody vine with tubular orange-and-red flowers in spring — one of the earliest hummingbird flowers. Semi-evergreen in mild climates. Climbs by tendrils with sticky pads.
A stunning native wildflower vine with extraordinary alien-looking purple-and-white flowers. Produces egg-sized edible fruits relished by mockingbirds, catbirds, and yellow-breasted chats.
Pennsylvania's state flower and one of the most spectacular native flowering shrubs, with intricate pink-and-white blossoms. Dense, evergreen growth provides year-round shelter for songbirds.

An iconic, long-lived evergreen oak of California's coast and valleys. Acorns are a critical food source for Acorn Woodpeckers, Band-tailed Pigeons, and Western Scrub-Jays. Supports 150+ caterpillar species.
The premier fall hummingbird plant for California and the West. Tubular scarlet flowers bloom from late summer through frost, providing critical nectar when Anna's Hummingbirds are most active.
